topic: Reqwest - Connection Timeouts vs Read Timeouts

turns.content: I'm stuck on a timeout issue in my new Rust project. I wrote this code where the request hangs forever even though I set a timeout, and I'm confused why it never returns an error: reqwest::Client::new().get("http://example.com").timeout(5).send()?.

That's a classic confusion. The `timeout` method on the request builder only applies to the read phase, not the connection phase. If your server takes too long to establish TCP, that `5 second` limit never kicks in because reqwest waits for the connection first. Think of it like ordering coffee: if the barista ignores you, you'll wait forever even if you set a timer on your watch; you only get the alarm when they finally hand you the cup.
So I need to use `connect_timeout` instead? But what happens after the connection is made?

Exactly. You need `connect_timeout` for the TCP handshake, and then `timeout` (or a separate read timeout) for data transfer. The distinction matters because they cover different parts of the network stack. If you only set one, you'll catch exactly what you're trying to avoid.
Okay, so how do I configure both in a single request?

You chain them on the client builder. Set `connect_timeout` first to limit handshake time, then add `.timeout()` for the read phase. This ensures you get errors from both phases instead of just hanging indefinitely.
Can I show this? I tried chaining them but got a compile error about the return type mismatch.

Ah, you're mixing up builder methods. `connect_timeout` returns a modified client, while `timeout` expects a request builder. You need to create separate requests or use the client's `get()` method and chain timeouts on that specific request object instead of the global client.
Right, so I should call `.connect_timeout()` on the client first, then `.timeout()` on the actual request. That makes sense now.

Perfect intuition. And remember to handle `reqwest::Error` properly in your match statement because both timeout types produce different error variants depending on which phase failed. This helps you distinguish between a slow server and a network drop.
One last thing, how do I set these values dynamically based on environment variables?

Use `std::env::var` to read the config strings, then parse them into `Duration` using `parse()`. Store those durations in a struct or closure so your request logic stays clean and reusable across different environments.

topic: Reqwest - Connection Timeouts vs Read Timeouts

turns.content: Hey, I'm building a side project this weekend and I've got reqwest working. I set connect_timeout on the client but I noticed it doesn't seem to cover read timeouts properly. Is there a better way to handle both connection failures and slow responses in one go?

Hey! That's a common confusion when migrating from curl or older libs. The connect_timeout only guards the TCP handshake, so if the server accepts but takes forever to send data, that timeout won't catch it. You need to combine both approaches. In reqwest you can set connect_timeout on the ClientBuilder and then add a read timeout by wrapping the request with RequestTimeout::from_secs(5) before calling run(). This ensures the connection attempt times out if too slow, but also stops waiting for body data if the stream stalls.
Got it. So I apply connect_timeout during client setup and then attach RequestTimeout to each request? What happens when reqwest returns an error after this?

Exactly, you build the Client with connect_timeout first, then for every request you wrap it in RequestTimeout. When either timeout fires, reqwest returns a specific error type instead of panicking or hanging forever. You catch that and decide whether to retry or show an alert to the user. The key insight is that these timeouts are independent; one protects the network layer while the other guards the payload stream.
That makes sense. But I'm seeing some cases where it still hangs even after adding the timeout. Is there something else I might be missing in how I handle errors?

Ah, you're likely dealing with socket reuse or connection pooling which can sometimes bypass simple timeouts if not configured carefully. Make sure your Client is fresh per request or that you're explicitly disabling keep-alive for this specific endpoint if needed. Also check if the timeout value exceeds the actual network latency on your machine; otherwise it might appear to hang because the system isn't yielding control fast enough.
I'll try disabling keep-alive for that endpoint then. One more thing though, if I want consistent behavior across multiple requests, should I configure this globally or per-request?

For most side projects, configuring it per-request is simpler and safer because different endpoints may have different latency requirements. If you have many similar calls, you can create a helper function that returns a Client with the desired timeouts pre-applied to reduce boilerplate. But remember that global settings won't override per-request constraints unless explicitly designed to.
Thanks! I'll refactor this into a utility function for my app. Just curious, does reqwest document the exact error types returned when these timeouts fire?

Yes, reqwest returns specific error variants like TimeoutError or RequestTimeout when either condition triggers. These are part of the standard error chain you can inspect to distinguish between connection failures and read delays. The documentation shows how to pattern match on these types so you can customize your retry logic accordingly.
